- rustiever: hey i would love to have some pointers to get started @andrewsomething
- andrewsomething: Hey @rustiever!

The  `doctl compute cdn flush` command is constructed here:

https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl/blob/76730b59c7349b665466ebbd687662709acd1baf/commands/cdns.go#L82-L92 

You can make updates to the help output there. Next, you can run `make build`. That will build a version of `doctl` with your changes in the `builds/` directory. You can check that your changes look as expected by running the command from the new build:

    ./builds/doctl compute cdn flush -h

Wildcards can only be used to recursively purge a folder, e.g.  `/spacename/foldername/*` or all files `*`, not for matching patterns ( `/spacename/*.obj`). So some example commands could be:

    doctl compute cdn flush <cdn-id>  --files /path/to/assets/*
    doctl compute cdn flush <cdn-id>  --files "/path/to/file.one, /path/to/file.two"
    doctl compute cdn flush <cdn-id>  --files /path/to/file.one --files  /path/to/file.two
    doctl compute cdn flush <cdn-id>  --files *

digitalocean/doctl#1020:
- rcj4747: This was introduced in https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl/commit/21279c8c5ab687508ec9130d5a7aa5695b460bfb then the TTL option was initially added.
- danaelhe: Thanks for bringing up this issue. You'll see that we've pushed up a PR for this issue #1027 . Once this PR is included in the latest doctl release, you'll need to do one more work around to get the expected behavior. The work around involves editing your doctl config file. Your doctl config file is located in different directories depending on your OS- you can see where your doctl config is located [here](https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl#configuring-default-values). Once you've located your doctl config file, edit the records.update.record-ttl from 1800 to 0, as shown below:
```
records:
  create:
    format: ""
    no-header: false
    record-data: ""
    record-flags: 0
    record-name: ""
    record-port: 0
    record-priority: 0
    record-tag: ""
    record-ttl: 1800
    record-type: ""
    record-weight: 0
  delete:
    force: false
  list:
    format: ""
    no-header: false
  update:
    format: ""
    no-header: false
    record-data: ""
    record-flags: 0
    record-id: 0
    record-name: ""
    record-port: 0
    record-priority: 0
    record-tag: ""
    record-ttl: 0
    record-type: ""
    record-weight: 0
```

This will get you the expected behavior.

digitalocean/doctl#857:
- andrewsomething: You need to pass the `--image`  when creating a Droplet. Public images have easy to remember "slugs" that you can reference, e.g.:

```
doctl compute droplet create \
  --image ubuntu-20-04-x64 \
  --size s-1vcpu-1gb \
  --region nyc1 \
  example.com
```

While private images like snapshots must be referenced by ID, eg:

```
doctl compute droplet create \
  --image 34566353 \
  --size s-1vcpu-1gb \
  --region nyc1 \
  example.com
```

You can find the ID's of your snapshots with: `doctl compute image list-user`
Or for public base images: `doctl compute image list-distribution`

Hope that answers your question. Let us know if you have any more!

digitalocean/doctl#709:
- andrewsomething: I wanted to provide a bit of an update here: As @gibiansky explained so well, the confinement provided by the Snap security model prevents `doctl` from calling out to the Docker binary. Additionally, the `docker` Snap interface only gives access to the Docker daemon, which does not provide the login mechanism. In order to address this, we have rewritten the command to not depend on the Docker binary being present (see: https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl/pull/805). This will still require us to get approval from the Canonical Snap team to get write access to the Docker configuration file (`~/.docker/config.json`). We're hopeful that this will be granted.

In the meantime, installing `doctl` directly is a workaround:

https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl#downloading-a-release-from-github
- braedon: Thanks for the update @andrewsomething.

Will #807 work when `~/.docker/config.json` is only accessible to root? Currently even installing `doctl` directly doesn't work unless the permissions on `~/.docker/config.json` are changed.
- andrewsomething: @braedon  Unfortunately, if  `~/.docker/config.json` is owned by `root` and `doctl` is run by a non-root user, it will not be able to access the file without changing its permissions. 

You might consider: https://docs.docker.com/engine/install/linux-postinstall/#manage-docker-as-a-non-root-user

digitalocean/doctl#670:
- kamaln7: If you run `doctl k8s cluster list --context my_own_account`, do you see the clusters on account A? Passing the `--context` flag to any command lets you specify the auth context to use instead of the default one.

If you saw your own clusters, you can change the default context from account T to account A by running: `doctl auth switch --context my_own_account`.

* The command returns an action ID that we can wait on (for example, something like a volume detach).
* The command doesn't return an action ID, but we can find one. An example here is a Droplet delete, where we can find the corresponding action through `doctl compute action list`.

- andrewsomething: Unassigning a floating IP returns an action item which can be used to check the the status. Fore example:

```
$ doctl compute floating-ip-action unassign 178.128.133.144
ID           Status         Type           Started At                       Completed At    Resource ID    Resource Type    Region
721743418    in-progress    unassign_ip    2019-07-15 22:00:12 +0000 UTC    <nil>           2994767248     floating_ip      nyc1
$ doctl compute action wait 721743418
ID           Status       Type           Started At                       Completed At                     Resource ID    Resource Type    Region
721743418    completed    unassign_ip    2019-07-15 22:00:12 +0000 UTC    2019-07-15 22:00:13 +0000 UTC    2994767248     floating_ip      nyc1
```

Using `wait` here will block until the action has completed. In a script, you could use something like:

```
ACTION_ID=`doctl compute floating-ip-action unassign --no-header --format ID ${FLOATING_IP}`
doctl compute action wait ${ACTION_ID}
```

digitalocean/doctl#479:
- bentranter: @hilary I'm not too sure. In our JSON displaying logic, we use `json.Marshal`, and the only option we get there is to either write `null` or nothing in the output, as there's no option for displaying `[]` instead of `null`.

With that said, another approach would be to check each `item` in the `writeJSON` function for any nil slices, and change them to empty slices before calling `json.Marshal`. That would require some pretty heavy use of the `reflect` package though, which I don't think we should get into.

Unfortunately, I think our best bet is to go through each of the commands, and check for cases where we're accidentally returning nil slices. It's the simplest solution, and after briefly looking last week when this issue was reported, I don't think there are too many cases where we do return nil slices.

On a different note, I wonder if fixing this issue qualifies as a breaking change. For example, if there are users who use a tool like `jq` to parse our JSON output, they're probably expecting `null` where we return it, and not `[]`.
- fleeto: But why do I saw `[]` in the load-balancer list?
- bentranter: @fleeto You were seeing `[]` in the load-balancer list due to an inconsistency in the way we rendered empty and nil slices. This is fixed as of [the latest release (1.22.0)](https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl/releases).

- andrewsomething: `doctl` [version 1.12.0](https://github.com/digitalocean/doctl/releases/tag/v1.12.0) was released with beta support for Kubernetes.

To enable beta features in `doctl`, you must first set the `DIGITALOCEAN_ENABLE_BETA` environmental variable to a non-zero value.

```
$ export DIGITALOCEAN_ENABLE_BETA=1
$ doctl kubernetes
[beta] kubernetes is used to access Kubernetes commands

Usage:
  doctl kubernetes [command]

Aliases:
  kubernetes, kube, k8s, k


Available Commands:
  get         get a cluster
  kubeconfig  get a cluster's kubeconfig file
  list        get a list of your clusters
  create      create a cluster
  update      update a cluster's properties
  delete      delete a cluster
  node-pool   node pool commands
  options     options commands

Flags:
  -h, --help   help for kubernetes

Global Flags:
  -t, --access-token string   API V2 Access Token
  -u, --api-url string        Override default API V2 endpoint
  -c, --config string         config file (default is $HOME/.config/doctl/config.yaml)
      --context string        authentication context name
  -o, --output string         output format [text|json] (default "text")
      --trace                 trace api access
  -v, --verbose               verbose output

Use "doctl kubernetes [command] --help" for more information about a command.
```

Here's an example of creating a cluster:

```
$ doctl k8s create \
   --name cluster-01 \
   --region nyc1 \
   --version 1.12.1-do.2 \
   --tag-names demo \
   --node-pools="name=worker-pool;size=s-1vcpu-1gb;count=3"

ID                                      Name          Region    Version        Status          Endpoint    IPv4    Cluster Subnet    Service Subnet    Tags                                                 Created At                       Updated At                       Node Pools
19b3bf99-cf14-46a3-a107-ea727ebd5a77    cluster-01    nyc1      1.12.1-do.2    provisioning                        10.244.0.0/16     10.245.0.0/16     demo,k8s,k8s:19b3bf99-cf14-46a3-a107-ea727ebd5a77    2018-11-27 15:54:13 +0000 UTC    2018-11-27 15:54:13 +0000 UTC    worker-pool
```

You can then grab the clusters kubeconfig using:

```
$ doctl k8s kubeconfig 19b3bf99-cf14-46a3-a107-ea727ebd5a77
```

There's still additional work planned. You can follow some of that in #372.

      --format string   Columns for output in a comma separated list. Possible values: ID,Name,Size,Region,Droplet IDs

digitalocean/doctl#288:
- xmudrii: Hi there,

You can use the output redirection to forward output to the file, for example:
```
doctl some-command > doctl-output.txt
```

To filter down the output and choose only needed columns, you can use the `--format` flag.
For example, the `doctl compute droplet list --format="Name,Memory,Disk,Status"` command would return something along:
```
Name                    Memory    Disk    Status
ubuntu-512mb-nyc3-01    512       20      active
ubuntu-512mb-nyc3-02    512       20      active
```

Append `> output.txt` to the end of the command, to get the `output.txt` file with same content as the above output.

You can also hide headers using the `--no-header` flag:
```
doctl compute droplet list --format="Name,Memory,Disk,Status" --no-header
```
```
ubuntu-512mb-nyc3-01    512    20    active
ubuntu-512mb-nyc3-02    512    20    active
```

This is the plain-text output and therefore, it could be harder to parse it. You can also output the command's result in JSON format, using the `--output` flag:
```
doctl compute droplet list --output=json
```
```
[                        
  {                                                                                                                                                                             
    "id": 78395000,              
    "name": "ubuntu-512mb-nyc3-01",

digitalocean/doctl#285:
- xmudrii: Hello @jonathantittle! :wave: 
This is intended behavior of `doctl` and API. Actually, the problem is that API is not documented properly.

API (and therefore `doctl`) has field `size_gigabytes`. However, [description](https://developers.digitalocean.com/documentation/v2/#create-a-new-block-storage-volume) states that size is in [gibibytes (GiB)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gibibyte).

1 gigabyte (GB) is 1000^3 bytes, while 1 gibibyte (GiB) is 1024^3 bytes.
When you're using API, it's assumed that you're providing size in gibibytes. You can also see it in the response—the result is 500 GiB volume.

With `doctl`, you're allowed to specify do you want 500 GB (465 GiB) or 500 GiB.
The correct `doctl` command is:
```
doctl compute volume create storage01-00 --region nyc3 --size 500GiB --desc storage01-00
```
I hope things are cleaner now. If you have any questions feel free to ping me.

digitalocean/doctl#184:
- xmudrii: Hi there! 
Can I help you with something specific?

**Version 1.5.0 and prior to 1.5.0** have following commands to help you create snapsohts:
## Droplet snapshots
### Creating snapshots
Command for creating snapshots is under `droplet-action` tree — `doctl compute droplet-action snapshot`.
**Syntax is following: `doctl compute droplet-action snapshot <droplet-id> --snapshot-name "some-name"`.**
Once executed, if successful, it'll return snapshot ID and other information:
```
ID		Status		Type		Started At			Completed At	Resource ID	Resource Type	Region
000000000	in-progress	snapshot	2017-01-25 16:35:15 +0000 UTC	<nil>		00000000	droplet		nyc1
```

### Listing snapshots
Command for Snapshots list is under `droplet` tree — `doctl compute droplet snapshots`.
It requires to provide you Droplet ID for which you want to see list of Snapshots. **Syntax is:
`doctl compute droplet snapshots <droplet-id>`.**
Sample output if operation is successful:
```
ID		Name	Type		Distribution	Slug	Public	Min Disk
000000000	test	snapshot	Ubuntu			false	20
```

### Removing snapshots
Command for removing Snapshots is located under `image` command tree — `doctl compute image delete`.
**Syntax is `doctl compute image delete <snapshot-id>`.**
In next step, you should be asked to confirm your intention. To do so, type 'y' and then press 'ENTER'. To skip confirmation you can use `force` flag:
`doctl compute image delete <snapshot-id> --force`.

### Renaming snapshots
Command for renaming is also located under `image` command tree — `doctl compute image update`.
**Syntax is `doctl compute image update <snapshot-id> --image-name "new-name"`.**
If successful, you should see list with that snapshot:
```
ID		Name		Type		Distribution	Slug	Public	Min Disk
000000000	new-name	snapshot	Ubuntu			false	20
```

## Volume (Block Storage) snapshots
**Commands for managing volumes snapshots are not available in version 1.5.0 or older!**

## Future of snapshot commands (development version)

As you can see, there are not in same place so it can be hard to keep up with it. To help users, new commands was implemented. Listing, getting and deleting snapshots is located in new command tree `snapshot`. Executing `doctl compute snapshot` will return you available actions:
```
snapshot is used to access snapshot commands

Usage:
  doctl compute snapshot [command]

Aliases:
  snapshot, s


Available Commands:
  list        list snapshots
  get         get snapshot
  delete      delete snapshot
```

Listing is done by executing: `doctl compute snapshot list`. It'll return list of snapshots for both Droplets and Volumes.
You can also get details of specific Droplet/Volume snapshot by executing: `doctl compute snapshot get <snapshot-id>`.
Deleting is done with: `doctl compute snapshot delete <snapshot-id>`. In next step you are asked to confirm intention. To do so, as already explained, type `y` and then press `ENTER`. Also you can use `--force` flag (and of this version shorthand flag `-f`).

Renaming and creating is where it was before.
Beside that you can also create Volume snapshot with `doctl compute volume snapshot <volume-id>`.
To get this version, you can build `doctl` from source, or wait for next release.

If you need anything more, feel free to ask me anything.

digitalocean/doctl#166:
- mchitten: Hey @xmudrii, thanks for asking. As you say, `--tag-name` (singular) is very similar to `--tag-names` (plural) except in a few ways:
1. `--tag-name` only accepts one tag.
2. `--tag-name` tag must already exist.
3. `--tag-names` accepts an array of tags.
4. tags in the `--tag-names` array _do not already have to exist_. If any tags in the array are new, we'll create them and apply them to the Droplet. If any tag names in the array already exist, we'll just apply them to the Droplet.

We didn't just outright do away with `--tag-name` because it would be a pretty sudden change, but we'll be deprecating it at some point soon. I highly recommend using `--tag-names` (plural) for the reasons and benefits described above.
- xmudrii: Thank you for explanation. I understand now why is it done like that and I agree on it. :)
As I understand it now, I'll close this issue.

digitalocean/doctl#145:
- xmudrii: Hi,

You must create tag before applying it to droplet.
`doctl compute droplet tag <IDNUM> --tag-name digitalocean` will apply **existing tag** with name `digitalocean` to your droplet.

`doctl compute tag list` will show all available tags you can attach to droplet with Droplet count, e.g. :

```
aaa     0
abb         0
abc         0
bac     0
```

So if you execute `doctl compute droplet tag <IDNUM> --tag-name aaa` it should do it successful, but if you execute `doctl compute droplet tag <IDNUM> --tag-name digitalocean` it will return 404 as `digitalocean` tag doesn't exist.

To create tag you can use command

```
doctl compute tag create sammy
```

Deleting tag could be done by

```
doctl compute tag delete sometag
```

Available tag commands are:

```
Usage:
  doctl compute tag [command]

Available Commands:
  create      create tag
  get         get tag
  list        list tags
  update      update tag
  delete      delete tag

Flags:
  -h, --help   help for tag

Global Flags:
  -t, --access-token string   API V2 Access Token
  -c, --config string         config file (default is $HOME/.config/doctl/config.yaml)
  -o, --output string         output format [text|json] (default "text")
      --trace                 trace api access
  -v, --verbose               verbose output

Use "doctl compute tag [command] --help" for more information about a command.
```

If you have problem beside that I can also recommend contacting support, because I think they can take closer look in your situation :)
- konstruktoid: Ah! Thanks alot @xmudrii, that worked wonders.
- zwhitchcox: So, in my opinion, this is a very unnecessarily complicated way to do this. Why can't we just add the tags via a string? What do we care what the id of a tag is? It seems like this should be a digital ocean back end thing that no one else ever has to worry about.

digitalocean/doctl#21:
- vit1251: Could you process auth without using X11 and browser?
- bryanl: Yes, if a `CLIAUTH` environment variable is set, it'll reply with a link you can visit in your browser. An easier method is to generate a token at https://cloud.digitalocean.com/settings/api/tokens, and add it to `$HOME/.doctlcfg` manually. The file format is yaml, so you'll add it like

``` yaml
access-token: <your token>
```

In a future version, there will be a command to manage configuration items.
- bryanl: We've decided to replace the `doctl auth login` with `doctl auth init`. It's being tracked in #94.

- IKEAMeatball: Unless I'm doing something wrong, I'm still getting this unknown client error, even with v1.1.0
- bryanl: @Phrown420 logging with the tool is a known issue on Windows. To proceed, generate an access token at https://cloud.digitalocean.com/settings/api/tokens. Then you have 3 options.
1. Add an `access-token` entry to `.doctlcfg` (this will be the easiest)
2. Use the access token argument when running `doctl`
3. Set `DIGITALOCEAN_ACCESS_TOKEN` environment variable to your token.
- bryanl: As I'm thinking right now `doctl auth login` will go away in the next release.  `doctl` will introduce something like `doctl auth init`, and that will require you to enter in your key.
